worked_examples.title: Adding rational numbers with unlike denominators
worked_examples.problem: Simplify: 3/4 + 5/6
steps: Find LCM of 4 and 6: LCM = 12
Convert fractions: 3/4 = 9/12 and 5/6 = 10/12

worked_examples.title: Finding a rational number between two numbers
worked_examples.problem: Find three rational numbers between 1/3 and 1/2.
steps: Convert to same denominator: 1/3 = 4/12, 1/2 = 6/12
Numbers between: 5/12 is one rational number
worked_examples.answer: 3/8, 5/12, 11/24 (many answers possible)

diagrams.title: Rational Numbers on a Number Line
Properties of Rational Numbers

worked_examples.title: Simplifying expressions with exponents
worked_examples.problem: Simplify: (2³ × 2⁴) ÷ 2⁵
steps: Apply product rule: 2³ × 2⁴ = 2^(3+4) = 2⁷
Apply quotient rule: 2⁷ ÷ 2⁵ = 2^(7-5) = 2²
Evaluate: 2² = 4

worked_examples.title: Expressing in standard form
worked_examples.problem: Write 0.0000472 in standard form.
steps: Move decimal point so there is one digit before it: 4.72
Count the places moved: 5 places to the right
Write as: 4.72 × 10⁻⁵

diagrams.title: Laws of Exponents

worked_examples.title: Finding square root by prime factorisation
worked_examples.problem: Find √324 using prime factorisation.

worked_examples.title: Checking if a number is a perfect square
worked_examples.problem: Is 720 a perfect square? If not, find the smallest multiplier to make it one.
steps: Prime factorise 720: 720 = 2⁴ × 3² × 5
For a perfect square, all prime factors must have even powers
5 has power 1 (odd) — not a perfect square
Multiply by 5 to make 5² : 720 × 5 = 3600
3600 = 2⁴ × 3² × 5² → perfect square (√3600 = 60)
worked_examples.answer: 720 is not a perfect square; multiply by 5 to get 3600

diagrams.title: Perfect Squares Table

worked_examples.title: Finding cube root by prime factorisation
worked_examples.problem: Find the cube root of 13824.
steps: Actually: 13824 = 2⁶ × 216 = 2⁶ × 6³

worked_examples.title: Checking if a number is a perfect cube
worked_examples.problem: Is 500 a perfect cube? Find the smallest number to multiply to make it a perfect cube.
steps: Prime factorise 500: 500 = 2² × 5³
For perfect cube, all prime factors need powers that are multiples of 3
2 has power 2 — need one more 2 to reach power 3
worked_examples.answer: Multiply by 2; 1000 is the perfect cube

diagrams.title: Perfect Cubes

worked_examples.title: Divisibility test
worked_examples.problem: Without actual division, check if 348762 is divisible by 6.
steps: Divisibility by 6 requires divisibility by both 2 and 3
By 2: last digit is 2 (even) → YES divisible by 2
By 3: sum of digits = 3+4+8+7+6+2 = 30; 30÷3=10 → YES divisible by 3
Since divisible by both 2 and 3, it is divisible by 6
worked_examples.answer: Yes, 348762 is divisible by 6

worked_examples.title: Finding the mystery number
worked_examples.problem: A two-digit number is 4 times the sum of its digits. If 18 is added to it, the digits interchange. Find the number.
steps: Let the number be 10a + b
Condition 1: 10a + b = 4(a + b) → 6a = 3b → b = 2a
Condition 2: 10a + b + 18 = 10b + a → 9a - 9b = -18 → b - a = 2
Substituting b = 2a: 2a - a = 2 → a = 2, b = 4
worked_examples.answer: The number is 24

diagrams.title: Divisibility Rules Quick Reference

worked_examples.title: Union and intersection of sets
steps: A∪B = all elements in A or B (no repeats) = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}
A∩B = elements in both A and B = {3, 4, 5}

worked_examples.title: Using sets to solve a real-life problem
worked_examples.problem: In a class of 40, 25 play cricket, 20 play football, and 10 play both. How many play neither?
steps: Let C = cricket players, F = football players
n(C∪F) = n(C) + n(F) - n(C∩F)
Neither = Total - n(C∪F) = 40 - 35 = 5
worked_examples.answer: 5 students play neither cricket nor football

diagrams.title: Venn Diagram — Sets A and B

worked_examples.title: Percentage increase
worked_examples.problem: A shirt costs ₹450. Its price is increased by 20%. What is the new price?
worked_examples.answer: New price = ₹540

worked_examples.title: Finding original value from percentage
worked_examples.problem: After a 15% discount, a book costs ₹425. What was the original price?
steps: Let original price = P
After 15% discount: P × (1 - 15/100) = 425
worked_examples.answer: Original price = ₹500

diagrams.title: Percentage ↔ Fraction ↔ Decimal Conversions

worked_examples.title: Finding profit percentage
worked_examples.problem: A trader buys goods for ₹3200 and sells them for ₹3840. Find the profit percentage.
worked_examples.answer: Profit percentage = 20%

worked_examples.title: Finding selling price given loss percentage
worked_examples.problem: A bicycle is bought for ₹2500. If it is sold at a loss of 12%, find the selling price.
worked_examples.answer: Selling price = ₹2200

worked_examples.title: Simple Interest calculation
worked_examples.problem: Find the SI on ₹8000 at 6.5% per annum for 3 years.
worked_examples.answer: Simple Interest = ₹1560; Amount = ₹9560

worked_examples.title: Compound Interest vs Simple Interest
worked_examples.problem: Find CI on ₹10,000 at 10% per annum for 2 years, compounded annually. Also find SI for comparison.
steps: CI Year 1 Interest = 10% of 10000 = ₹1000 → Amount = ₹11000
CI Year 2 Interest = 10% of 11000 = ₹1100 → Amount = ₹12100
worked_examples.answer: SI = ₹2000; CI = ₹2100; CI is ₹100 more

diagrams.title: SI vs CI Comparison

worked_examples.title: Direct variation problem
worked_examples.problem: If 8 workers can build a wall in 12 days, how many workers are needed to build it in 6 days?
steps: More workers → fewer days → INVERSE variation
Workers × Days = constant
worked_examples.answer: 16 workers are needed

worked_examples.title: Direct proportion table
worked_examples.problem: A car travels 240 km in 4 hours at constant speed. Fill in the table: distance for 1, 2, 3, 6 hours.
steps: Speed = 240/4 = 60 km/h (constant)
1 hour: 60 km | 2 hours: 120 km | 3 hours: 180 km | 6 hours: 360 km
Ratio Distance/Time = 60 = constant → direct proportion

diagrams.title: Direct vs Inverse Variation

worked_examples.title: Adding and subtracting algebraic expressions
worked_examples.problem: Simplify: (3x² + 5x - 2) + (2x² - 3x + 7) - (x² - x + 1)
steps: Group like terms: x² terms: 3x² + 2x² - x² = 4x²
x terms: 5x - 3x - (-x) = 5x - 3x + x = 3x
Constants: -2 + 7 - 1 = 4

worked_examples.title: Multiplying algebraic expressions
worked_examples.problem: Find the product: (2x + 3)(x² - x + 4)
steps: Distribute 2x: 2x·x² - 2x·x + 2x·4 = 2x³ - 2x² + 8x
Distribute 3: 3·x² - 3·x + 3·4 = 3x² - 3x + 12
worked_examples.answer: 2x³ + x² + 5x + 12

diagrams.title: Algebraic Expression Structure

worked_examples.title: Using the identity (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b²
worked_examples.problem: Expand (3x + 4y)²
steps: Use identity: (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b²
Here a = 3x, b = 4y
worked_examples.answer: 9x² + 24xy + 16y²

worked_examples.title: Using identity to compute 101²
worked_examples.problem: Find the value of 101² using an algebraic identity.

diagrams.title: Key Algebraic Identities

worked_examples.title: Factorising using common factors
worked_examples.problem: Factorise: 12x³y - 18x²y² + 6xy³
steps: Find HCF of terms: HCF(12,18,6)=6, HCF(x³,x²,x)=x, HCF(y,y²,y³)=y → HCF = 6xy
Divide each term by 6xy:
12x³y ÷ 6xy = 2x² | -18x²y² ÷ 6xy = -3xy | 6xy³ ÷ 6xy = y²
Factorised form: 6xy(2x² - 3xy + y²)
worked_examples.answer: 6xy(2x² - 3xy + y²)

worked_examples.title: Factorising using the difference of squares
worked_examples.problem: Factorise: 25a² - 64b²
steps: Recognise as a² - b² form
25a² = (5a)² and 64b² = (8b)²
Apply: a² - b² = (a+b)(a-b)

diagrams.title: Methods of Factorisation

worked_examples.title: Solving a linear equation
steps: Expand: 3x - 12 - 4x + 10 = 4 - x
Simplify LHS: -x - 2 = 4 - x
Add x to both sides: -2 = 4 (contradiction!)
Actually check: -x - 2 = 4 - x → -x + x = 4 + 2 → 0 = 6 (no solution)
worked_examples.answer: No solution (inconsistent equation)

worked_examples.title: Word problem — age
worked_examples.problem: Priya is 5 years older than Raj. Six years from now, the sum of their ages will be 43. Find their current ages.
steps: Let Raj's current age = x, so Priya's age = x + 5
In 6 years: Raj = x+6, Priya = x+11
worked_examples.answer: Raj is 13, Priya is 18

diagrams.title: Steps to Solve a Linear Equation

worked_examples.title: Solving a linear inequation
worked_examples.problem: Solve 3x - 5 > 10 and show on a number line. x ∈ ℝ.
steps: Add 5 to both sides: 3x > 15
Divide by 3: x > 5
Solution set: x ∈ (5, ∞)
Number line: open circle at 5, arrow pointing right →

worked_examples.title: Double inequation
worked_examples.problem: Solve -3 ≤ 2x + 1 < 7, x ∈ ℤ. List integer solutions.
steps: Subtract 1 from all parts: -4 ≤ 2x < 6
Divide all parts by 2: -2 ≤ x < 3
Integer values: x = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2

diagrams.title: Inequation Number Line Notation

worked_examples.title: Angle sum of a quadrilateral
worked_examples.problem: Three angles of a quadrilateral are 70°, 115°, and 90°. Find the fourth angle.
steps: Sum of angles of a quadrilateral = 360°
worked_examples.answer: Fourth angle = 85°

worked_examples.title: Properties of a parallelogram
worked_examples.problem: In parallelogram ABCD, ∠A = 65°. Find all other angles.
steps: ∠A + ∠B = 180° (co-interior angles) → ∠B = 115°
∠C = ∠A = 65° (opposite angles are equal)
∠D = ∠B = 115° (opposite angles are equal)

diagrams.title: Types of Quadrilaterals

worked_examples.title: Perimeter of a rhombus
worked_examples.problem: The diagonals of a rhombus are 16 cm and 12 cm. Find the perimeter.
steps: Diagonals of rhombus bisect each other at right angles
Half-diagonals: 8 cm and 6 cm
Side of rhombus = √(8² + 6²) = √(64+36) = √100 = 10 cm
Perimeter = 4 × 10 = 40 cm
worked_examples.answer: Perimeter = 40 cm

worked_examples.title: Area of a trapezium
worked_examples.problem: A trapezium has parallel sides 13 cm and 9 cm and height 6 cm. Find its area.
steps: Area of trapezium = ½ × (sum of parallel sides) × height
worked_examples.answer: Area = 66 cm²

diagrams.title: Special Quadrilateral Properties

worked_examples.title: Constructing a quadrilateral given 4 sides and a diagonal
worked_examples.problem: Describe the steps to construct quadrilateral ABCD where AB=5cm, BC=4.5cm, CD=3.5cm, DA=4cm, AC=6cm.
steps: Draw AC = 6 cm
With A as centre and radius 5 cm, draw arc; with C as centre and radius 4.5 cm, draw arc → they meet at B
With A as centre and radius 4 cm, draw arc; with C as centre and radius 3.5 cm, draw arc → they meet at D
Join AB, BC, CD, DA to complete the quadrilateral
worked_examples.answer: Quadrilateral ABCD is constructed

worked_examples.title: Constructing a parallelogram
worked_examples.problem: Construct parallelogram ABCD with AB=6cm, BC=4.5cm and ∠ABC=75°.
steps: Draw AB = 6 cm
At B, draw ray at 75° to AB; mark C so BC = 4.5 cm
At A, draw ray parallel to BC; at C, draw ray parallel to AB
The intersection point is D
worked_examples.answer: Parallelogram ABCD constructed

diagrams.title: Geometric Construction Tools and Their Uses

worked_examples.title: Identifying nets of 3D shapes
worked_examples.problem: How many faces does a cuboid have? Describe one possible net.
steps: A cuboid has 6 rectangular faces
A net is formed by unfolding — lay down one large face, attach faces to each of its 4 sides, one more on top
Net looks like a cross shape or T shape with 6 rectangles
worked_examples.answer: 6 faces; net is a cross-shaped arrangement of 6 rectangles

worked_examples.title: Counting vertices, edges and faces
worked_examples.problem: A triangular prism has 5 faces. Verify Euler's formula: F + V - E = 2.
steps: Faces (F) = 2 triangular + 3 rectangular = 5
Vertices (V) = 6 (3 on each triangle)
Edges (E) = 9 (3 on each triangle + 3 lateral)
worked_examples.answer: Euler's formula verified: 5 + 6 - 9 = 2

diagrams.title: Common 3D Shapes: F, V, E

worked_examples.title: Line symmetry
worked_examples.problem: How many lines of symmetry does a regular hexagon have?
steps: A regular hexagon has 6 equal sides and 6 equal angles
Lines of symmetry: 3 through opposite vertices + 3 through midpoints of opposite sides
Total = 6 lines of symmetry
worked_examples.answer: 6 lines of symmetry

worked_examples.title: Rotational symmetry
worked_examples.problem: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a square?
steps: Rotate square by 90°, 180°, 270°, 360°
At each of these angles it looks the same
Order = 4 (it maps to itself 4 times in one full turn)
worked_examples.answer: Order of rotational symmetry = 4, angle = 90°

diagrams.title: Lines of Symmetry for Regular Polygons

worked_examples.title: Area of a trapezium
worked_examples.problem: ABCD is a trapezium with AB ∥ CD. AB=15cm, CD=8cm, height=10cm. Find area.
steps: Area = ½ × (AB + CD) × h
worked_examples.answer: Area = 115 cm²

worked_examples.title: Area of a polygon by splitting into triangles
worked_examples.problem: Find the area of a regular hexagon with side 6 cm.
steps: Split hexagon into 6 equilateral triangles each with side 6 cm
Area of one equilateral triangle = (√3/4) × 6² = 9√3 cm²
Total area = 6 × 9√3 = 54√3 ≈ 93.5 cm²
worked_examples.answer: Area ≈ 93.5 cm²

diagrams.title: Area Formulae for Polygons

worked_examples.title: Surface area of a cuboid
worked_examples.problem: Find the total surface area and volume of a cuboid 12cm × 8cm × 5cm.
steps: Volume = l × b × h = 12 × 8 × 5 = 480 cm³
worked_examples.answer: TSA = 392 cm², Volume = 480 cm³

worked_examples.title: Volume of a cylinder
worked_examples.problem: A cylindrical tank has radius 7 m and height 10 m. Find its volume and capacity in litres.
steps: 1 m³ = 1000 litres
worked_examples.answer: Volume = 1540 m³; Capacity = 15,40,000 litres

diagrams.title: Surface Area and Volume Formulae

worked_examples.title: Mean, median and mode
worked_examples.problem: Find mean, median and mode of: 4, 7, 3, 9, 7, 5, 7, 4, 8, 6
steps: Median = average of 5th and 6th values = (6+7)/2 = 6.5
Mode = 7 (appears 3 times)
worked_examples.answer: Mean = 6, Median = 6.5, Mode = 7

worked_examples.title: Reading a histogram
worked_examples.problem: A histogram shows: 0-10: 5 students, 10-20: 12, 20-30: 18, 30-40: 10, 40-50: 5. How many students scored more than 20?
steps: Students scoring 20-30: 18
Students scoring 30-40: 10
Students scoring 40-50: 5
Total above 20 = 18 + 10 + 5 = 33
worked_examples.answer: 33 students scored more than 20

diagrams.title: Measures of Central Tendency

worked_examples.title: Basic probability
worked_examples.problem: A bag has 3 red, 5 blue, and 2 green balls. A ball is drawn at random. Find P(red), P(not red).
steps: Total balls = 3 + 5 + 2 = 10
worked_examples.answer: P(red) = 3/10; P(not red) = 7/10

worked_examples.title: Probability with a die
worked_examples.problem: A fair die is rolled. Find the probability of getting (a) a prime number (b) a multiple of 3.
steps: Sample space = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}, n(S) = 6
Prime numbers in S: {2, 3, 5} → P(prime) = 3/6 = 1/2
Multiples of 3 in S: {3, 6} → P(mult of 3) = 2/6 = 1/3
worked_examples.answer: P(prime) = 1/2; P(multiple of 3) = 1/3

diagrams.title: Probability Scale

worked_examples.title: Explaining Brownian motion
worked_examples.problem: Smoke particles suspended in air are seen to move randomly under a microscope. Explain why using KTM.
steps: According to KTM, air molecules are in constant random motion
They collide with smoke particles from all directions
These collisions are unequal and random
The net unbalanced force on the smoke particle changes direction constantly
This produces the observed random (Brownian) motion
worked_examples.answer: Random air molecule collisions cause random net forces on smoke particles = Brownian motion

worked_examples.title: Temperature and KE relationship
worked_examples.problem: Why does a gas exert more pressure when its temperature is increased at constant volume?
steps: Higher temperature → greater kinetic energy of gas molecules (KTM)
Molecules move faster and collide with container walls more frequently
Each collision also carries more momentum
More frequent + more forceful collisions = greater pressure
worked_examples.answer: Higher T → higher KE → more/harder wall collisions → greater pressure

diagrams.title: States of Matter — KTM Comparison
Brownian Motion

worked_examples.title: Converting units
worked_examples.problem: A table is 1.8 m long. Express in (a) cm (b) mm (c) km

worked_examples.title: Calculating density
worked_examples.problem: A metal block of mass 390 g has dimensions 5cm × 6cm × 4cm. Find its density.
steps: Volume = 5 × 6 × 4 = 120 cm³
Density = Mass/Volume = 390/120 = 3.25 g/cm³
In SI: 3.25 × 1000 kg/m³ = 3250 kg/m³
worked_examples.answer: Density = 3.25 g/cm³ = 3250 kg/m³

diagrams.title: SI Base Units

worked_examples.title: Calculating pressure
worked_examples.problem: A box weighing 60 N rests on a floor. Its base is 30 cm × 20 cm. Find the pressure on the floor.
steps: Area = 30 cm × 20 cm = 600 cm² = 0.06 m²
Pressure = Force / Area = 60 N / 0.06 m²
worked_examples.answer: Pressure = 1000 Pa

worked_examples.title: Why pressure varies with area
worked_examples.problem: A woman weighing 500 N wears high heels (tip area = 2 cm²). A man of same weight wears flat shoes (area = 100 cm²). Compare pressures.
steps: Pressure (heels) = 500 N / 0.0002 m² = 2,500,000 Pa
Pressure (flat shoes) = 500 N / 0.01 m² = 50,000 Pa
Heels exert 50× more pressure
worked_examples.answer: High heels exert 50 times more pressure — that's why they sink into soft ground

diagrams.title: Pressure, Force, and Area Triangle

worked_examples.title: Calculating kinetic energy
worked_examples.problem: A car of mass 1200 kg moves at 20 m/s. Find its kinetic energy.
steps: KE = ½ mv²

worked_examples.title: Conservation of energy
worked_examples.problem: A ball of mass 0.5 kg is dropped from rest at a height of 10 m. Find its speed just before hitting the ground. (g = 10 m/s²)
steps: At top: PE = mgh = 0.5 × 10 × 10 = 50 J; KE = 0
At bottom: all PE converts to KE (no air resistance)
KE = 50 J → ½ mv² = 50
worked_examples.answer: Speed just before impact ≈ 14.1 m/s

diagrams.title: Energy Conversion at Different Heights

worked_examples.title: Reflection by a plane mirror
worked_examples.problem: A ray of light strikes a plane mirror at 35° to the normal. What is the angle of reflection? What is the total angle through which the ray is deviated?
steps: By law of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection = 35°
Total deviation = 180° - (i + r) = 180° - 70° = 110°
worked_examples.answer: Angle of reflection = 35°; Total deviation = 110°

worked_examples.title: Image in a plane mirror
worked_examples.problem: An object stands 30 cm in front of a plane mirror. Where is the image? State its characteristics.
steps: Image distance = object distance = 30 cm (behind the mirror)
Image is virtual (cannot be formed on screen)
Image is erect (same orientation)
Image is laterally inverted (left-right swapped)
Image is the same size as the object
worked_examples.answer: Image: 30 cm behind mirror, virtual, erect, laterally inverted, same size

diagrams.title: Reflection of Light — Plane Mirror

worked_examples.title: Identifying modes of heat transfer
worked_examples.problem: Classify each as conduction, convection, or radiation: (a) heat from sun (b) hot air from heater rising (c) metal spoon getting hot in soup
steps: (a) Sun → Earth through vacuum — cannot be conduction or convection → Radiation
(b) Hot air rises (fluid movement) → Convection
(c) Heat travels through solid metal spoon → Conduction
worked_examples.answer: a) Radiation  b) Convection  c) Conduction

worked_examples.title: Good and poor conductors
worked_examples.problem: A saucepan is made of steel with a plastic handle. Explain why each material was chosen.
steps: Steel body: good conductor — transfers heat from flame to food quickly
Plastic handle: poor conductor (insulator) — does not transfer heat to user's hand
This design combines thermal efficiency with safety
worked_examples.answer: Steel (good conductor) heats food; plastic (insulator) protects hand

diagrams.title: Three Modes of Heat Transfer

worked_examples.title: Calculating speed of sound
worked_examples.problem: Thunder is heard 4 seconds after lightning is seen. How far away is the storm? (Speed of sound = 340 m/s)
steps: Distance = Speed × Time
worked_examples.answer: The storm is 1360 m (1.36 km) away

worked_examples.title: Echo calculation
worked_examples.problem: To hear an echo, the minimum distance from a reflecting wall is? (Speed of sound = 340 m/s; persistence of hearing = 0.1 s)
steps: For an echo, the reflected sound must reach the ear 0.1 s after the original
Total distance (to wall + back) = 340 × 0.1 = 34 m
Minimum distance from wall = 34/2 = 17 m
worked_examples.answer: Minimum distance = 17 m

diagrams.title: Properties of Sound

worked_examples.title: Ohm's Law calculation
worked_examples.problem: A bulb has resistance 12 Ω and is connected to a 6V battery. Find the current through it.
steps: By Ohm's Law: V = IR
worked_examples.answer: Current = 0.5 A (or 500 mA)

worked_examples.title: Series and parallel circuits
worked_examples.problem: Two resistors 4Ω and 6Ω are connected (a) in series, (b) in parallel. Find combined resistance in each case.
steps: Series: Rₛ = R₁ + R₂ = 4 + 6 = 10 Ω
worked_examples.answer: Series: 10 Ω; Parallel: 2.4 Ω

diagrams.title: Circuit Symbols
Series vs Parallel Circuits

worked_examples.title: Identifying a pure substance
worked_examples.problem: How would you distinguish between a pure substance and a mixture? Give one test for each.
steps: Pure substance: has a fixed, sharp melting/boiling point
Example: pure water boils at exactly 100°C at 1 atm
Mixture: boiling point varies as composition changes
Example: saltwater boils above 100°C and the b.p. rises as more water evaporates
worked_examples.answer: Pure substances have fixed boiling points; mixtures have variable boiling points

worked_examples.title: Classification of matter
worked_examples.problem: Classify: (a) Seawater (b) Distilled water (c) Air (d) Diamond
steps: Seawater → contains water, salt, dissolved gases → Mixture
Distilled water → only H₂O → Pure substance → Compound
Air → N₂, O₂, CO₂, Ar etc. → Mixture
Diamond → only carbon atoms → Pure substance → Element
worked_examples.answer: Seawater: mixture | Distilled water: compound | Air: mixture | Diamond: element

diagrams.title: Classification of Matter

worked_examples.title: Identifying type of change
worked_examples.problem: State whether each is a physical or chemical change, with reason: (a) melting of ice (b) burning of paper (c) dissolving sugar in water
steps: (a) Ice → water: same substance (H₂O), reversible → Physical change
(b) Paper + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + ash: new substances formed → Chemical change
(c) Sugar dissolves but is still sugar (recover by evaporation) → Physical change
worked_examples.answer: a) Physical  b) Chemical  c) Physical

worked_examples.title: Signs of chemical change
worked_examples.problem: You mix baking soda and vinegar. List 3 signs that prove it is a chemical change.
steps: Vigorous bubbling of gas (CO₂ produced)
Temperature change (slight cooling)
New substances formed: sodium acetate + water + CO₂
The change is NOT easily reversed
worked_examples.answer: Gas production, temperature change, new substances formed, irreversible

diagrams.title: Physical vs Chemical Change

worked_examples.title: Separating a mixture
worked_examples.problem: You have a mixture of iron filings, salt, and sand. Describe the steps to separate them.
steps: Step 1: Use a magnet to attract and remove iron filings
Step 2: Add remaining salt+sand mixture to water → salt dissolves, sand does not
Step 3: Filter to separate sand from salt solution
Step 4: Evaporate the salt solution to get pure salt
worked_examples.answer: Magnet → filtration → evaporation gives all three pure components

worked_examples.title: Comparing element, compound, mixture
worked_examples.problem: Water (H₂O), hydrogen gas (H₂), and a hydrogen-oxygen gas mixture — compare them.
steps: H₂ → only hydrogen atoms, simplest pure substance → Element
H₂O → H and O chemically combined in 2:1 ratio, fixed properties → Compound
H₂+O₂ gas mixture → physically mixed, any ratio, properties of both → Mixture
worked_examples.answer: H₂: element | H₂O: compound | H₂+O₂: mixture

diagrams.title: Element vs Compound vs Mixture

worked_examples.title: Electronic configuration
worked_examples.problem: Write the electronic configuration of sodium (Na, atomic number 11). Identify the number of valence electrons.
steps: Shell capacity: K=2, L=8, M=18
Na has 11 electrons: K=2, L=8, M=1
Electronic configuration: 2,8,1
Valence electrons = electrons in outermost shell = 1
worked_examples.answer: Na: 2,8,1 → 1 valence electron

worked_examples.title: Protons, neutrons, electrons
worked_examples.problem: Chlorine-35 has atomic number 17. Find (a) protons (b) electrons (c) neutrons
steps: Atomic number = number of protons = 17
In a neutral atom: electrons = protons = 17
Mass number = protons + neutrons → 35 = 17 + N → N = 18
worked_examples.answer: Protons = 17, Electrons = 17, Neutrons = 18

diagrams.title: Bohr Model of Sodium (Na)
Sub-atomic Particles

worked_examples.title: Writing chemical formulae
worked_examples.problem: Write the formula for calcium chloride and explain how you determined it.
steps: Calcium (Ca) has valency 2; Chloride (Cl) has valency 1
Criss-cross method: Ca₁Cl₂ → CaCl₂
Interpretation: 1 Ca²⁺ ion combines with 2 Cl⁻ ions to give neutral compound
worked_examples.answer: CaCl₂

worked_examples.title: Balancing a chemical equation
worked_examples.problem: Balance: Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃
steps: Unbalanced: Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃
Balance Fe: need 4Fe to give 2 Fe₂O₃ → 4Fe + O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃
Balance O: 2Fe₂O₃ has 6 O atoms → 3O₂ on left
Balanced: 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃
worked_examples.answer: 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃

diagrams.title: Common Ion Valencies

worked_examples.title: Identifying type of reaction
worked_examples.problem: Classify: (a) 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO (b) CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ (c) AgNO₃ + NaCl → AgCl + NaNO₃
steps: (a) Mg combines with O₂ → one product → Combination (synthesis) reaction
(b) CaCO₃ breaks into two → Decomposition reaction
(c) Two compounds swap partners → Double displacement reaction
worked_examples.answer: a) Combination  b) Decomposition  c) Double displacement

worked_examples.title: Oxidation and reduction
worked_examples.problem: In CuO + H₂ → Cu + H₂O, identify what is oxidised and what is reduced.
steps: CuO → Cu: Cu loses oxygen → CuO is REDUCED; Cu is the product
H₂ → H₂O: H₂ gains oxygen → H₂ is OXIDISED
CuO is the oxidising agent (it oxidises H₂)
H₂ is the reducing agent (it reduces CuO)
worked_examples.answer: H₂ is oxidised; CuO is reduced; This is a redox reaction

diagrams.title: Types of Chemical Reactions

worked_examples.title: Allotropes of carbon
worked_examples.problem: Diamond and graphite are both made of only carbon. Why are their properties so different?
steps: Diamond: each C bonds to 4 others in a 3D tetrahedral network → very hard, no free electrons → non-conductor
Graphite: each C bonds to 3 others in 2D layers → weak van der Waals between layers → soft, slippery
Graphite has free electrons in each layer → good conductor
worked_examples.answer: Different crystal structures cause completely different properties — this is allotropy

worked_examples.title: Combustion of carbon
worked_examples.problem: Write equations for complete and incomplete combustion of carbon.
steps: Complete combustion (excess O₂): C + O₂ → CO₂ (carbon dioxide)
Incomplete combustion (limited O₂): 2C + O₂ → 2CO (carbon monoxide, toxic)
CO is toxic because it binds to haemoglobin instead of O₂
worked_examples.answer: Complete: C + O₂ → CO₂ | Incomplete: 2C + O₂ → 2CO

diagrams.title: Allotropes of Carbon

worked_examples.title: Hard and soft water
worked_examples.problem: Hard water does not lather well with soap. Why? How can temporary hardness be removed?
steps: Hard water contains dissolved Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ salts
These react with soap to form scum (insoluble salts): Ca²⁺ + 2RCOONa → Ca(RCOO)₂ ↓ + 2Na⁺
Temporary hardness is due to dissolved Ca(HCO₃)₂
Removed by: boiling → Ca(HCO₃)₂ → CaCO₃ ↓ + H₂O + CO₂
worked_examples.answer: Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ ions react with soap; boiling removes temporary hardness

worked_examples.title: Electrolysis of water
worked_examples.problem: When water is electrolysed, what is produced at each electrode? What is the ratio of gases?
steps: Water: H₂O (+ dilute H₂SO₄ to conduct)
At cathode (−): 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂ (hydrogen gas)
At anode (+): 2OH⁻ → H₂O + ½O₂ + 2e⁻ → O₂ (oxygen gas)
Volume ratio H₂ : O₂ = 2 : 1 (confirms formula H₂O)
worked_examples.answer: Cathode: hydrogen; Anode: oxygen; Ratio H₂:O₂ = 2:1

diagrams.title: Water Treatment Steps

worked_examples.title: Laboratory preparation of hydrogen
worked_examples.problem: Write the equation for preparation of H₂ from zinc and dilute H₂SO₄. State the test for hydrogen gas.
steps: Equation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂ ↑
Test: Bring a burning splint near the mouth of the collection tube
Result: If H₂ is present, it burns with a 'pop' sound
worked_examples.answer: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂; Test = squeaky pop with burning splint

worked_examples.title: Properties of hydrogen
worked_examples.problem: Why is hydrogen gas collected by upward displacement of air (downward delivery)?
steps: Hydrogen is the lightest gas (density ≈ 0.09 g/L, air ≈ 1.29 g/L)
H₂ is 14 times lighter than air
It rises and collects at the top of the collection container
Gas jar must be inverted (open end down) to collect H₂
worked_examples.answer: H₂ is lighter than air, so it rises → collected by upward displacement

diagrams.title: Properties of Hydrogen

worked_examples.title: Osmosis demonstration
worked_examples.problem: A potato piece is placed in a strong salt solution. What happens and why?
steps: Salt solution has lower water potential (more solute) than potato cells
Water moves by osmosis from high concentration (potato cells) to low (salt solution)
Potato cells lose water → potato becomes soft and shrunken (plasmolysis)
worked_examples.answer: Potato shrinks — water leaves cells by osmosis into the concentrated salt solution

worked_examples.title: Transpiration and its importance
worked_examples.problem: Why is transpiration considered both a necessary evil and beneficial?
steps: Necessary evil: plants lose 97% of absorbed water via transpiration (wasteful)
Beneficial: creates the transpiration pull that draws water up from roots
Also: cools the plant surface and helps mineral absorption
worked_examples.answer: Transpiration drives water movement upward despite causing water loss

diagrams.title: Transport in Plants

worked_examples.title: Types of asexual reproduction
worked_examples.problem: Name the type of asexual reproduction in each: (a) Amoeba (b) Yeast (c) Bryophyllum (d) Potato
steps: (a) Amoeba → divides in two → Binary fission
(b) Yeast → produces buds → Budding
(c) Bryophyllum → leaf buds develop into new plants → Vegetative propagation (leaves)
(d) Potato → eyes (buds) grow into new plants → Vegetative propagation (stem)
worked_examples.answer: a) Binary fission  b) Budding  c) Leaf propagation  d) Stem (tuber) propagation

worked_examples.title: Pollination vs Fertilisation
worked_examples.problem: Distinguish between pollination and fertilisation in flowering plants.
steps: Pollination: transfer of pollen grains from anther to stigma of a flower
Fertilisation: fusion of male gamete (from pollen) with female gamete (in ovule)
Pollination comes first → pollen tube grows → fertilisation occurs
worked_examples.answer: Pollination = pollen transfer; Fertilisation = gamete fusion (inside ovule)

diagrams.title: Parts of a Flower and Their Functions

worked_examples.title: Comparing asexual and sexual reproduction
worked_examples.problem: State TWO advantages of sexual reproduction over asexual reproduction.
steps: 1. Genetic variation: offspring are not identical → better adaptation to changing environments
2. Eliminates harmful mutations: two parents → bad genes may be masked by dominant alleles
3. Asexual is faster but produces clones with no variation
worked_examples.answer: 1. Produces genetic variation 2. Allows evolution and adaptation

worked_examples.title: Internal vs External fertilisation
worked_examples.problem: Why do frogs (external fertilisation) lay thousands of eggs, while humans (internal fertilisation) have very few offspring?
steps: External fertilisation: eggs/sperm released into water → many lost to environment
Very low chance of fertilisation per egg → must produce thousands
Internal fertilisation: sperm directly deposited near eggs → high fertilisation chance
Better protection of embryo → fewer offspring needed
worked_examples.answer: External fertilisation has low success rate → more eggs needed; internal is efficient → fewer

diagrams.title: Sexual vs Asexual Reproduction

worked_examples.title: Food chain and energy flow
worked_examples.problem: In a food chain: Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake → Eagle. If grass has 10,000 J, how much energy reaches the eagle? (10% rule)
steps: Grasshopper receives 10% of 10,000 = 1,000 J
Frog receives 10% of 1,000 = 100 J
Snake receives 10% of 100 = 10 J
Eagle receives 10% of 10 = 1 J
worked_examples.answer: Eagle gets only 1 J out of 10,000 J (0.01%)

worked_examples.title: Biotic and abiotic factors
worked_examples.problem: List 3 abiotic factors that affect a forest ecosystem.
steps: Sunlight: drives photosynthesis, controls which plants grow
Temperature: affects metabolic rates and species distribution
Rainfall/Water: essential for all life processes
(Also valid: soil type, humidity, wind)
worked_examples.answer: Sunlight, temperature, rainfall (and soil, humidity, etc.)

diagrams.title: Energy Flow in an Ecosystem

worked_examples.title: Hormone action
worked_examples.problem: During a stressful situation, what hormone is released and what are its effects?
steps: Adrenal gland releases ADRENALINE (epinephrine)
Effects: heart rate ↑, breathing rate ↑, pupils dilate, blood to muscles ↑
Blood glucose rises (glycogen → glucose), digestive system slows
Body prepares for 'fight or flight' response
worked_examples.answer: Adrenaline → 'fight or flight': faster heart + breathing, dilated pupils, more blood to muscles

worked_examples.title: Feedback mechanism
worked_examples.problem: Explain how blood glucose levels are regulated (negative feedback).
steps: After meal: blood glucose rises
Pancreas detects rise → releases INSULIN
Insulin stimulates cells to absorb glucose; glucose converted to glycogen
Blood glucose falls back to normal → insulin secretion stops
worked_examples.answer: Insulin brings high glucose down; glucagon brings low glucose up — this is negative feedback

worked_examples.title: Changes during puberty
worked_examples.problem: List 3 physical changes that occur in both males and females during puberty.
steps: 1. Growth spurt: rapid increase in height
2. Appearance of pubic and underarm hair
3. Skin becomes oilier (more sebaceous gland activity), risk of acne
worked_examples.answer: Growth spurt, body hair, oily skin (common to both)

worked_examples.title: Role of sex hormones
worked_examples.problem: Which hormone is responsible for voice changes in boys? Which gland produces it?
steps: Hormone: Testosterone (male sex hormone)
Produced by: Testes (male gonads)
Effect on voice: larynx grows → vocal cords lengthen → voice deepens ('voice breaks')
worked_examples.answer: Testosterone from testes causes the larynx to grow, deepening the voice

diagrams.title: Changes During Puberty

worked_examples.title: Tracing blood flow
worked_examples.problem: Trace the path of a red blood cell from the right atrium to the lungs and back to the left ventricle.
steps: Right atrium → Right ventricle (through tricuspid valve)
Right ventricle → Pulmonary artery (through pulmonary valve)
Pulmonary artery → Lungs (O₂ absorbed, CO₂ released)
Lungs → Pulmonary veins → Left atrium → Left ventricle
worked_examples.answer: RA → RV → Pulmonary artery → Lungs → Pulmonary veins → LA → LV

worked_examples.title: Arteries vs Veins
worked_examples.problem: State 3 differences between arteries and veins.
steps: 1. Arteries carry blood AWAY from heart; veins carry blood TO heart
2. Arteries carry oxygenated blood (except pulmonary artery); veins carry deoxygenated (except pulmonary vein)
3. Arteries have thick, elastic walls; veins have thin walls with valves
worked_examples.answer: 1. Direction (away vs toward heart) 2. O₂ content 3. Wall thickness/valves

diagrams.title: Double Circulation in Humans

worked_examples.title: Reflex arc
worked_examples.problem: You touch a hot plate and immediately withdraw your hand. Trace the reflex arc.
steps: Receptor (skin): detects heat/pain
Sensory (afferent) neuron: carries impulse to spinal cord
Relay neuron in spinal cord: processes and redirects impulse (no brain involvement)
Motor (efferent) neuron: carries impulse to effector
Effector (hand muscles): contracts, withdrawing hand
worked_examples.answer: Receptor → Sensory neuron → Spinal cord (relay) → Motor neuron → Muscle

worked_examples.title: Structure of a neuron
worked_examples.problem: Name and describe the parts of a motor neuron.
steps: Cell body: contains nucleus, where the neuron 'lives'
Dendrites: branching fibres that receive nerve impulses
Axon: long fibre that carries impulse away from cell body
Myelin sheath: fatty covering that speeds up impulse
Synaptic knobs: endings that release neurotransmitters to next neuron
worked_examples.answer: Cell body, dendrites (receive), axon (transmit), myelin sheath, synaptic knobs

diagrams.title: Reflex Arc

worked_examples.title: Communicable vs non-communicable
worked_examples.problem: Classify: (a) Tuberculosis (b) Diabetes (c) Cholera (d) Cancer
steps: (a) TB: caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, spreads via air → Communicable
(b) Diabetes: metabolic disorder, not infectious → Non-communicable
(c) Cholera: caused by Vibrio cholerae, spreads via contaminated water → Communicable
(d) Cancer: uncontrolled cell growth, not infectious → Non-communicable
worked_examples.answer: Communicable: TB, Cholera | Non-communicable: Diabetes, Cancer

worked_examples.title: First aid for bleeding
worked_examples.problem: State the correct first-aid steps for a deep cut on the arm that is bleeding.
steps: 1. Apply direct pressure with clean cloth/bandage to stop bleeding
2. If cloth soaks through, add another on top (do not remove first cloth)
3. Raise the injured arm above heart level to reduce blood flow
4. Bandage firmly and seek medical help immediately
worked_examples.answer: Pressure → elevate → bandage → seek medical help

diagrams.title: Pathogens and the Diseases They Cause

worked_examples.title: Improving crop yield
worked_examples.problem: A farmer's rice yield is low. List THREE scientific methods to improve it.
steps: 1. Use of high-yielding variety (HYV) seeds from selective breeding
2. Proper use of fertilisers (NPK) and irrigation for adequate nutrients and water
3. Crop protection: use of pesticides and crop rotation to prevent pests and diseases
worked_examples.answer: HYV seeds, fertilisers/irrigation, pest control

worked_examples.title: Difference between manure and fertiliser
worked_examples.problem: Compare manure and chemical fertilisers for agricultural use.
steps: Manure: natural (decomposed organic matter), slow-acting, improves soil structure
Fertiliser: artificial (chemically made), fast-acting, may damage soil long-term
Manure = less risk of pollution; fertiliser = targeted nutrients but risk of leaching
worked_examples.answer: Manure: natural, slow, eco-friendly | Fertiliser: artificial, fast, targeted but risks pollution

worked_examples.title: Example: Present Tenses (1)
worked_examples.problem: Simple Present use
worked_examples.steps: Complete: She _____ (go) to school every day. They _____ (not like) cold food.
answer: 'Every day' = habitual → Simple Present
She goes to school every day.
They don't like cold food.

worked_examples.title: Example: Present Tenses (2)
worked_examples.problem: Choosing correct tense
worked_examples.steps: Choose: I _____ here since 2010. (live/have lived/am living)
answer: 'Since 2010' = started in past, still ongoing → Present Perfect
→ have lived

diagrams.title: Present Tense Forms

worked_examples.title: Example: Past Tenses (1)
worked_examples.problem: Simple Past vs Past Perfect
worked_examples.steps: Complete: By the time the police arrived, the thief _____ (escape).
answer: Past Perfect used for action completed BEFORE another past action
'arrived' is simple past → escape happened first
→ had escaped

worked_examples.title: Example: Past Tenses (2)
worked_examples.problem: Narrative past tense
worked_examples.steps: Write one sentence for each: Simple Past, Past Continuous, Past Perfect.
answer: Simple Past: She walked to school.
Past Continuous: She was walking to school when it rained.
Past Perfect: She had already left when he called.

worked_examples.title: Example: Future Tenses (1)
worked_examples.problem: Will vs going to
worked_examples.steps: Choose: Look at those clouds! It _____ rain. I _____ call you tomorrow.
answer: 'Look at those clouds' = evidence/plan → going to
'Call you tomorrow' = promise/decision → will

worked_examples.title: Example: Future Tenses (2)
worked_examples.problem: Future Progressive
worked_examples.steps: Make a sentence using future continuous for: study / 8 PM
answer: Future continuous = will be + V-ing
She will be studying at 8 PM.

worked_examples.title: Example: Sentences — Types and Structure (1)
worked_examples.problem: Classify by purpose
worked_examples.steps: Classify: 1. What a beautiful painting! 2. Close the door. 3. Is anyone home? 4. The earth is round.
answer: 1. Exclamatory (strong emotion, exclamation mark)
2. Imperative (command/request)
3. Interrogative (question, question mark)
4. Declarative/Assertive (statement)

worked_examples.title: Example: Sentences — Types and Structure (2)
worked_examples.problem: Subject and predicate
worked_examples.steps: Identify subject and predicate: 'The little girl in the red dress laughed loudly.'
answer: Subject = 'The little girl in the red dress' (who laughed?)
Predicate = 'laughed loudly' (what did she do?)

diagrams.title: Types of Sentences by Purpose

worked_examples.title: Example: Simple, Compound, Complex Sentences (1)
worked_examples.problem: Combine using conjunctions
worked_examples.steps: Join: 'She was tired. She kept working.' Use: (a) though (b) but
answer: (a) Though she was tired, she kept working. (Complex — subordinate clause)
(b) She was tired, but she kept working. (Compound — coordinating conjunction)

worked_examples.title: Example: Simple, Compound, Complex Sentences (2)
worked_examples.problem: Identify clause type
worked_examples.steps: Identify as simple/compound/complex: 'He runs fast because he trains daily.'
answer: Has 2 clauses: 'He runs fast' (main clause) + 'because he trains daily' (subordinate clause)
Main + subordinate clause → Complex sentence

diagrams.title: Sentence Types by Structure

worked_examples.title: Example: Subject-Verb Agreement (1)
worked_examples.problem: Correcting errors
worked_examples.steps: Correct: 'Neither of the boys have finished their work.' Why?
answer: 'Neither' is singular (refers to one at a time)
Singular subject → singular verb
Correct: Neither of the boys has finished his work.

worked_examples.title: Example: Subject-Verb Agreement (2)
worked_examples.problem: Collective nouns
worked_examples.steps: Choose correct verb: 'The team _____ (is/are) confident of winning.'
answer: Collective noun 'team': in British English, can be singular OR plural depending on unity
If team acting as one → singular: The team is confident.
Both are accepted in standard usage

worked_examples.problem: Topic sentence and support
worked_examples.steps: Write a topic sentence and 2 supporting sentences for the topic 'The importance of reading'.
answer: Topic sentence: Reading is one of the most valuable habits a person can develop.
Support 1: Regular reading expands vocabulary and improves language skills.
Support 2: It also broadens one's knowledge and cultivates the imagination.

worked_examples.title: Example: Composition and Paragraph Writing (2)
worked_examples.problem: Paragraph structure
worked_examples.steps: Arrange in correct paragraph order: (a) evidence, (b) topic sentence, (c) concluding sentence.
answer: Correct order: (b) Topic sentence → states the main idea
Then (a) Evidence → supports the idea with facts or examples
Finally (c) Concluding sentence → wraps up and reinforces the point

worked_examples.problem: Choose correct preposition

'9 AM' = specific time → at
'an hour' = period of time → after or within

worked_examples.title: Example: Prepositions (2)
worked_examples.problem: Phrasal verbs with prepositions
worked_examples.steps: Explain: 'She gave up her studies' vs 'She gave away her books'.

gave away = donated freely (away changes meaning of give)
These are phrasal verbs: verb + preposition/particle = new meaning

worked_examples.problem: Write the key differences between formal and informal letters.
steps: Formal: to unknown/official person; professional language; no contractions; proper salutation (Dear Sir/Madam)
Informal: to friends/family; casual language; contractions allowed; personal tone (Dear Mum)
worked_examples.answer: Formal = official/professional; Informal = personal/casual

worked_examples.problem: Identify which is a formal opening and which is informal: (a) 'Dear Mr. Smith' (b) 'Hi Tom!'

(b) 'Hi Tom!' — informal (first name + casual greeting)
worked_examples.answer: (a) Formal  (b) Informal

worked_examples.title: Example 1: 12 Determiners
worked_examples.problem: Choose the correct determiner: 'I read _____ book last night. It was _____ interesting story.'
steps: 'book' — specific book being referred to → 'the'
'interesting story' — first mention, starts with vowel sound → 'an'
worked_examples.answer: I read the book last night. It was an interesting story.

worked_examples.title: Example 2: 12 Determiners
worked_examples.problem: Correct the errors: 'There are few water left. I have less friends than her.'
steps: 'water' is uncountable → use 'little' (not few)
'friends' is countable → use 'fewer' (not less)
Correct: There is little water left. I have fewer friends than her.
worked_examples.answer: little water; fewer friends

worked_examples.title: Example 1: 13 Conjunctions
worked_examples.problem: Join using the correct conjunction: 'She studied hard. She failed.' (Hint: unexpected result)
steps: Unexpected result between two clauses → use 'yet' or 'but'
She studied hard, yet she failed. / She studied hard, but she failed.
worked_examples.answer: She studied hard, yet she failed. (adversative conjunction)

worked_examples.title: Example 2: 13 Conjunctions
worked_examples.problem: Identify conjunction type: 'I will come if you invite me.'
steps: 'if' introduces a condition → subordinating conjunction of condition
It links main clause 'I will come' with subordinate clause 'if you invite me'
worked_examples.answer: Subordinating conjunction (conditional)

diagrams.title: Types of Conjunctions

worked_examples.title: Example 1: 14 Active and Passive Voice Part 1
worked_examples.problem: Convert to passive: 'The chef cooked the meal.'
steps: Identify: subject=chef (actor), verb=cooked, object=meal
Passive: Object becomes subject → 'The meal'
Tense (simple past) → was/were + V₃
The meal was cooked by the chef.
worked_examples.answer: The meal was cooked by the chef.

worked_examples.title: Example 2: 14 Active and Passive Voice Part 1
worked_examples.problem: Convert to active: 'A letter was written by Sara.'
steps: Agent (by Sara) becomes subject: Sara
Past passive (was written) → simple past active: wrote
Object: a letter
worked_examples.answer: Sara wrote a letter.

diagrams.title: Active to Passive Formula by Tense

worked_examples.title: Example 1: 15 Active and Passive Voice Part 2
worked_examples.problem: Convert to passive: 'People speak English all over the world.'
steps: Impersonal passive: no agent needed
Subject 'people' becomes agent (often omitted)
Passive: English is spoken all over the world. (no 'by people' needed)
worked_examples.answer: English is spoken all over the world.

worked_examples.title: Example 2: 15 Active and Passive Voice Part 2
worked_examples.problem: Convert: 'She was given a prize by the principal.' Is this correct passive?
steps: Two objects possible: direct (prize) and indirect (her)
Both can become passive subject
More natural: A prize was given to her by the principal.
Or: She was given a prize by the principal. (both correct)
worked_examples.answer: Both passives are correct; indirect object as subject is most natural

diagrams.title: Passive Voice: Special Cases

worked_examples.problem: Write the format of a formal notice for a school event.
steps: Title: NOTICE (centred, bold)
Organisation/School name at top

Heading: clear title of notice
Body: brief, clear, who/what/when/where/how
Issued by: Name and designation

worked_examples.title: Example 2: 16 Notice Email Writing Diary Writing
worked_examples.problem: What are the key differences between a formal email and a letter?
steps: Email: has To/From/Subject/CC fields; no postal address needed
Email: immediate delivery, often shorter, less formal than letters
Letter: physical format, more formal structure, used for official records
worked_examples.answer: Email is faster, digital, with To/Subject fields; letter is formal, physical

worked_examples.problem: Fill in with appropriate conjunctions: 'I would have called _____ I had your number. He is poor _____ honest.'
steps: First blank: condition (would have → if)
Second blank: contrast → yet/but
I would have called if I had your number. He is poor yet honest.

worked_examples.problem: Combine using correlative conjunctions: 'Sara plays tennis. She plays badminton. (both...and)'

worked_examples.title: Example 1: 19 Summary and Precis Writing Figures of Speech Story Writing
worked_examples.problem: Summarise in one sentence: 'Every morning, the villagers gathered at the well. They chatted, laughed, and shared news. The well was not just a source of water — it was the heart of the community.'
steps: Main idea: the well was the social centre of village life
Summary: The village well served as both a water source and the social hub of the community.
worked_examples.answer: The village well served as the social hub of the community.

worked_examples.title: Example 2: 19 Summary and Precis Writing Figures of Speech Story Writing
worked_examples.problem: Identify the figure of speech: (a) 'The wind howled all night.' (b) 'Life is a journey.' (c) 'Her voice was as sweet as honey.'
steps: (a) Wind 'howled' (human action) → Personification
(b) Life = journey (direct comparison without like/as) → Metaphor
(c) Voice compared to honey using 'as' → Simile
worked_examples.answer: a) Personification  b) Metaphor  c) Simile

diagrams.title: Common Figures of Speech

worked_examples.title: Example 1: 20 Reported Speech Part 1
worked_examples.problem: Convert to indirect speech: 'She said, "I am very happy."'
steps: Reporting verb 'said' (no object) — no change needed
Remove quotation marks, add 'that'
Change pronoun 'I' → 'she' (to match original speaker)
Change 'am' → 'was' (backshift: present → past)
worked_examples.answer: She said that she was very happy.

worked_examples.title: Example 2: 20 Reported Speech Part 1
worked_examples.problem: Convert: 'The teacher asked, "Have you done your homework?"'
steps: Question → reported with 'if/whether'
Remove question mark, use statement word order
Tense backshift: have done → had done
The teacher asked if/whether I had done my homework.
worked_examples.answer: The teacher asked if/whether I had done my homework.

diagrams.title: Tense Backshift in Reported Speech

worked_examples.title: Example 1: 21 Reported Speech Part 2
worked_examples.problem: Convert: 'He said, "Please help me."' (request)
steps: Request/order → use 'asked/requested + to-infinitive'
He requested me to help him.
worked_examples.answer: He requested/asked me to help him.

worked_examples.title: Example 2: 21 Reported Speech Part 2
worked_examples.problem: Convert: 'She exclaimed, "What a wonderful view!"'
steps: Exclamatory sentence → use 'exclaimed that'
She exclaimed that it was a wonderful view.
worked_examples.answer: She exclaimed that it was a wonderful view.

diagrams.title: Reporting Different Sentence Types

worked_examples.title: Example 1: 22 Modals
worked_examples.problem: Choose the correct modal: 'You _____ wear a seatbelt. It's the law. (must/should)'
steps: 'Must' = obligation (legal requirement)
'Should' = advice (weaker)
Legal requirement → must
worked_examples.answer: You must wear a seatbelt.

worked_examples.title: Example 2: 22 Modals
worked_examples.problem: Rewrite using a modal to show possibility: 'Perhaps she is at home.'
steps: 'Perhaps' suggests possibility → use 'might' or 'may'
She might be at home. / She may be at home.
worked_examples.answer: She might/may be at home.

diagrams.title: Modal Verbs and Their Meanings

worked_examples.title: Example 1: 23 Transformation of Sentences Part 1
worked_examples.problem: Transform: 'No other city in India is as big as Mumbai.' (Comparative degree)
steps: 'No other... as... as' = Superlative idea
Comparative: Mumbai is bigger than any other city in India.
worked_examples.answer: Mumbai is bigger than any other city in India.

worked_examples.title: Example 2: 23 Transformation of Sentences Part 1
worked_examples.problem: Transform into negative without changing meaning: 'She is always late.'
steps: Always = 'never on time'
She is never on time.
worked_examples.answer: She is never on time.

diagrams.title: Degree Transformations

worked_examples.title: Example 1: 24 Transformation of Sentences Part 2
worked_examples.problem: Transform to exclamatory: 'It is a very cold day.'
steps: Remove 'very', add 'What a' or 'How'
What a cold day it is! / How cold the day is!
worked_examples.answer: What a cold day it is! / How cold it is!

worked_examples.title: Example 2: 24 Transformation of Sentences Part 2
worked_examples.problem: Transform to complex sentence: 'Being tired, she slept early.'
steps: 'Being tired' = participial phrase → convert to subordinate clause
Because she was tired, she slept early.
worked_examples.answer: Because she was tired, she slept early.

diagrams.title: Transformation Patterns

worked_examples.title: Example 1: 25 Phrases and Clauses
worked_examples.problem: Identify: 'The girl with the red hat is my sister.'
steps: 'with the red hat' — has no verb → Phrase (adjective phrase modifying 'girl')
No separate subject + verb combination within 'with the red hat'
worked_examples.answer: 'with the red hat' is an adjective phrase

worked_examples.title: Example 1: 26 Articles
worked_examples.problem: Fill in the blanks: 'I saw ___ elephant at ___ zoo. ___ elephant was eating ___ banana.'
steps: First mention 'elephant' → a (but starts with vowel sound) → an
'zoo' = specific place → the
Second mention (specific elephant) → the
'banana' = first mention, not specific → a
worked_examples.answer: an elephant; the zoo; The elephant; a banana

worked_examples.title: Example 2: 26 Articles
worked_examples.problem: When do we NOT use an article? Give 2 examples.
steps: No article with: proper nouns (London, India), uncountable used generally (Water is life), plural used generally (Dogs are loyal)
Example 1: India is a beautiful country. (proper noun)
Example 2: Gold is precious. (uncountable noun, general meaning)
worked_examples.answer: Proper nouns and uncountable/plural nouns used in general sense take no article

diagrams.title: Article Usage Guide

worked_examples.title: Example 1: 27 Homonyms Homophones
worked_examples.problem: Distinguish: 'bear' as homonym in two sentences.
steps: bear (noun) = the animal: A bear roared in the forest.
bear (verb) = to tolerate: I cannot bear the noise.
Same spelling, same pronunciation, different meanings → Homonym
worked_examples.answer: Homonyms: same spelling AND pronunciation, different meanings

worked_examples.title: Example 2: 27 Homonyms Homophones
worked_examples.problem: Use correctly: 'their/there/they're'
steps: their = possessive (belonging to them): Their house is big.
there = place or introductory: There is a cat there.
they're = they are: They're coming tonight.
worked_examples.answer: their (possession), there (place/intro), they're (contraction of they are)

NaCl: Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions (charges of ±1)
